course_description:
This course aims to develop students' ability to manage and retrieve information from Information Systems. Students will learn how to use Structured Query Language (SQL) to retrieve information critical for business decision making, and will also develop a thorough understanding of data integrity and its effects on the quality of business decisions. The course also provides a hands-on experience with conceptual modelling of data requirements for business. This course further enhances the students' employability in other areas such as the ability to work in teams, the ability to think analytically and critically, and build confidence in presenting professionally.

This course provides students with an overall understanding of the relational database model. It emphasises the SQL query language and seeks to develop students' ability to design and retrieve information from relational databases. The course informs students of database elements including domains, nulls, recovery, concurrency, security, and other similar issues. As such, students will learn how to: use the SQL query language to retrieve relevant information for business purposes; construct semantic data models to help achieve business objectives and know how to convert these models into normalized tables; evaluate and apply integrity constraints including domains, primary keys, and foreign keys; understand the concept of nulls and how this concept applies to SQL queries and database design; understand the importance of the database elements in their application; gain an appreciation of the benefits and requirements of distributed databases.

Students will learn to use AI tools such as Gemini and ChatGPT to support code generation, prompt engineering, and the validation of SQL queries. They will also learn to transform complex database questions into accurate SQL queries through AI-guided exploration and develop the skills needed to use AI responsibly in data management.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

course_aims: This course aims to provide students with an overall understanding of the relational database model. In doing so, it emphasises the SQL query language and seeks to develop each student's ability to design and retrieve information from relational databases. The course also aims to make students aware of domains, nulls, recovery, concurrency, security, and other similar issues. This course further enhances the students' employability in other areas such as the ability to workﾠin teams, the ability to thinkﾠanalyticallyﾠand critically, and build confidence in presenting professionally.

learning_outcomes.description: Apply data modelling techniques to design effective data structures tailored to specific business contexts.
Apply SQL to extract data from relational information systems, and apply generative AI tools responsibly to support database design, coding and validation.
Analyse data quality issues and implement data cleaning and validation techniques to enhance data integrity and reliability.
Research and understand current Information Systems issues and identify how to exploit available data for corporate advantage.
Collaborate effectively in team environments to communicate technical information clearly through written and oral presentations, essential for successful information systems development projects.

assessment_details.title: System Development
assessment_details.due_date: 22/05/2026 4:00 pmPeer Assessment due 1 week after the assessment due date.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed, Longitudinal.See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The main purpose of this assessment is to develop your ability to work in a team and to design, develop, and implement a well-structured relational database that effectively supports the given scenario. Students will be required to demonstrate their knowledge and ability to use SQL. The fundamental knowledge and skills required for this assessment task are expected to be cultivated through lectures and tutorials. However, students will need to conduct additional research to further enhance the skills necessary to complete this assessment.Team formation and teamwork:You will undertake this project as part of a team of 4-5.Project team members must be allocated to the same tutorial. You will submit portions of the project at intervals, receive oral comments on your work during the informal presentations from your tutor, and revise earlier submissions.Iteration Session (Week 8):Students will submit selected sections of their project by the end of Week 8. During the week 9 tutorial, students receive oral comments on their work from their tutor. This iteration submission is for feedback only and is not graded.Peer Assessment:students will be required to complete a self and peer evaluation as part of this assignment. The evaluation process assists the teaching team in understanding how each student contributed and the findings are confidential. The peer evaluation can affect each student's mark for this assignment. A link will be provided on Blackboard for this evaluation.Working effectively in a Team:A Practical Guide MOOC explores ways in which to learn how to build effective teams, be a great team player, and manage team conflict. You are encouraged to complete this practical guide if you haven't done a team project before. Refer toTeams101x MOOC.AI Statement:Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.Read full task descriptionHide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on theUQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Course Overview and Intro to DBMSIntroduction to database management, including traditional file-based systems, the database management system and fundamental database concepts.Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 1, 2)Learning outcomes:L01, L04

Entity-Relationship ModelEntity relationship modelling is explored in terms of a data-driven approach using entity-relationship diagrams, including design elements such as entities, relationships, attributes, keys and multiplicity.Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 12 and 13)Learning outcomes:L01, L04

T1: Data & Infomration and SQL prepStudents will explore the difference between data and information, examine the 3-level database architecture through discussion and case analysis, and begin preparing for practical database work by setting up and navigating the MySQL environment.Team formation during the tutorialLearning outcomes:L01, L04
Relational ModelRelational model concepts are discussed including specific terminology, keys, views and integrity constraints.Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 4)

T2: Entity-Relationship ModelStudents will identify entities and relationships in real-world scenarios, review key concepts such as multiplicity and participation constraints, and apply their understanding through practical ER modelling exercises.Learning outcomes:L01

Database Design and Structured Query Language (I)Introduction to fundamental SQL Data Definition Language (DDL) and Data Manipulation Language (DML) statements.Readings/References:CB, Chapters 6 and 7

T3: Relational ModelStudents will work through practical exercises on relations, keys, and integrity constraints, and apply these concepts by creating a relational model and corresponding relational schema.
Structured Query Language (II)Exploring aggregation and grouping, and relational operations to enable search conditions and order/grouping of results.Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 6,7)

T4: SQL ExercisesThe tutorial focuses on foundational SQL skills through practical exercises in MySQL, starting with basic queries and manual command-line operations. Students will then engage in AI-assisted prompt engineering to generate, interpret, and refine SQL queries.
In-Semester QuizIn Semester exam during Lecture Time.

T5: SQL ExercisesThe tutorial focuses on foundational SQL skills through practical exercises in MySQL, starting with basic queries and manual command-line operations. Students will then engage in AI-assisted prompt engineering to generate, interpret, and refine SQL queries.
In-Semester Break

NormalisationExplore how normalisation supports database design and how concepts such as partial, transitive, and functional dependencies enable the logical database design to transition and conform to particular normal forms.Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 14,15)

T6: SQL ExercisesThis tutorial focuses on advancing SQL skills using MySQL Workbench, with an emphasis on tackling complex queries and clarifying challenging concepts. Students will also explore AI-assisted prompt engineering to generate and refine advanced SQL queries, enhancing their problem-solving and query optimization abilities.

Data Quality and Cleaning, and Presentation SkillsData is a valuable resource and access to accurate, current and informative data is critical to business success. Therefore, data needs to be managed and organised like other business assets.

T7:  Normalisation ExercisesStudents will engage in practical exercises to identify functional dependencies and apply normalization techniques up to 3NF, reinforcing their understanding of data structure optimization and redundancy reduction.
Guest LectureTBALearning outcomes:L01, L04

T8:  Assignment Iteration feedback and Data cleaningThis tutorial focuses on providing feedback on Assignment 1 iterations and developing data cleaning skills. Students will identify and resolve common data quality issues to prepare datasets for accurate analysis and reporting.

Transaction ManagementThis lecture explores the critical role of transaction management in business systems, focusing on how organisations maintain data integrity and consistency in multi-user environments and recover from failures to ensure reliable operations.Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 22)Learning outcomes:L01, L04
T9: Team projectThis tutorial is dedicated to the team project, providing students with structured time to work in their groups, develop project deliverables.

Database Security and ControlWe’ll look at why database security matters, the main threats to data security, and the core ideas of confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the CIA triad). We’ll also cover practical countermeasures and finish with an overview of Data Control Language (DCL).Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 20)

T10: Database security and team projectThis tutorial covers practical exercises in database security, focusing on access control and user permissions. Students will also continue working on their team projects.
Distributed Database Management SystemsWe examine distributed DBMSs including concepts, problems, technologies and protocols.Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 24, 25)Learning outcomes:L01, L04
T11: Team ProjectThis tutorial is dedicated to the team project, providing students with structured time to work in their groups, develop project deliverables, and receive feedback on their progress.
Big Data and Intelligence TechnologiesExploring why data management technologies and approaches have expanded beyond relational databases and data warehousing.Learning outcomes:L01, L04
T12: Q&A and Exam PracticeExam consult and practice
